About Me

Software & Machine Learning Engineer

Hello and welcome to my portfolio! I'm a software and machine learning engineer who enjoys building intelligent systems and the full-stack applications that put them to work. My most recent work spans anomaly detection, retrieval-augmented generation, and turning research ideas into reliable, production-ready tools. I graduated Summa Cum Laude from Cleveland State University in May 2026 with a Bachelor of Science in Computer Science.

Career

Professional Experience

Software Engineering Co-op

nVent
May 2025 - December 2025 Solon, OH
  • Trained and tested a Convolutional Autoencoder with PyTorch to detect anomalous activity in electrical waveforms, achieving a 93% detection rate on real datasets containing electrical arcs
  • Engineered an anomaly scoring and thresholding pipeline using reconstruction error in Python, reducing false positives by 95% across all test cases
  • Ran Bayesian hyperparameter optimization with Optuna, significantly cutting experimentation time and achieving a high-performance model with fewer training runs
  • Parsed Node.js server logs and designed a dashboard using React.js and TailwindCSS to track test outcomes, improving testing and documentation efficiency
  • Maintained a Jira automation tool tracking 1000+ projects, saving the company $100k+ in annual labor costs

Software Development Intern

VN Services
June 2024 - December 2024 Chesterland, OH
  • Designed and implemented a full-stack web application using Next.js and Flask capable of securely hosting and querying relational databases stored with SQLite
  • Containerized the application with Docker and deployed to AWS Fargate (ECS), configuring task definitions, networking, and environment variables to enable cloud-based data hosting
  • Implemented REST API endpoints supporting role-based authentication, CRUD operations, and input validation, improving app security and enforcing consistent API contracts
  • Improved the backend features of an internal training platform, implementing server-side changes to support dynamic content delivery and an interactive user experience
  • Orchestrated an automation workflow that periodically synchronized database updates with existing visualizations, improving the consistency of observed metrics

Projects

Featured Projects

A selection of projects that I chose for a reason. Each solves a problem that I believe matters, avoids well-trodden ground, and produces verifiable results during every stage of development.

Retrieval and Memory

Cross-Modal RAG System

A local-first memory engine that turns long-lived personal artifacts (notes, code, images, and PDF files) into a queryable, time-aware system. Every answer stays tied to retrievable source evidence, with an explicit citation you can open and verify. Everything runs locally, with no cloud dependency by default.

RAGEmbeddingsFAISSLocal-FirstOCR
LLMs and Formal Methods

Improving LLM Reasoning via Autoformalization

My senior design project done with a team of 4 CS students. We evaluated and improved how well flagship large language models (LLMs) convert natural-language mathematics into verifiable LEAN 4 code, then wrapped the method in an accessible natural-language interface for formal theorem proving.

LLMsLEAN 4LangChainRAGTool Use
Deep Learning

FSM Anomaly Detection

Unsupervised runtime fault detection in finite state machines (FSMs). A dense autoencoder trained only on normal behavior flags anomalous state sequences, measured against a rule-based transition whitelist across three FSMs and three fault types.

View all projects on GitHub
Retrieval and Memory

Cross-Modal RAG System

View on GitHub

CrossModalRAG builds a hierarchical, time-aware memory on top of standard Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG). It answers questions about your own past work and grounds every claim in a specific source location you can open and check.

The problem

Standard RAG indexes text chunks and retrieves them by semantic similarity. That approach breaks down for long-running personal memory for three reasons: 1) context spreads across notes, commits, screenshots, and documents; 2) the same concept changes over time; and 3) old information does not all deserve equal weight. CrossModalRAG answers this by building a time-aware, hierarchical memory that keeps a strict link from every claim back to its evidence.

Cross-modal ingestion

I extended the ingestion pipeline so images and PDF files become first-class evidence alongside notes and code. A PDF chunk carries its page number, so a citation can render as 'file.pdf p.4'. An image is registered as a source and made searchable through local optical character recognition (OCR), recording region coordinates when the engine supplies them. The source fingerprint includes the OCR engine version, so re-ingesting an unchanged file does nothing, while an engine upgrade re-derives only what it affects.

Hierarchical memory model

Above the raw evidence layer, the system derives three higher levels: atomic events, episodes that group those events, and durable concepts. These levels answer synthesis questions such as 'how did my understanding of chunking change?' that flat retrieval cannot. Each higher node links down to at least one citable evidence chunk, so no abstraction loses its provenance. Derivation runs on a local model at temperature zero and skips any input whose fingerprint has not changed.

Grounded retrieval and answering

A single query returns mixed evidence types (text, code, image, and PDF) and reranks them with awareness of their modality. Generation is constrained to a claim-then-evidence format with explicit source identifiers, and it reports when the evidence is too thin instead of inventing facts. Image-native embeddings stay behind a benefit threshold fixed in advance, so the system adopts them only when measurement justifies the added complexity.

Engineering discipline

The system runs locally and stays light on dependencies. OCR, PDF parsing, and image-embedding backends install as optional extras, and the core package works without them, skipping a modality with a clear message when its extra is absent. Cross-modal evidence reuses the existing database tables through new field values rather than a schema redesign. Retrieval quality is measured by Recall@K, Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR), and citation hit-rate.

RAGEmbeddingsFAISSLocal-FirstOCR
LLMs and Formal Methods

Improving LLM Reasoning via Autoformalization

An online, natural-language interface that shows the autoformalization (AF) abilities of flagship large language models (LLMs), paired with a system that proves symbolic equivalence and measures semantic consistency. To our knowledge, it is the first such system built for the LEAN 4 proof assistant.

Motivation

LLM adoption has grown fast, yet the reasoning often fails under close inspection, behaving more like probabilistic pattern-matching than formal logic. Theorem proving sits upstream of that reasoning and demands real rigor: reading a statement precisely, tracking many claims at once, and deriving new claims from old ones. Formal proof systems help mathematical research but stay niche and hard to use, so the project set out to lower that barrier and to measure how well current models formalize.

What the project tackled

Theorem proving splits into autoformalization (AF), automatic theorem proving, and informalization. This project focused on proposition AF, the step that turns a natural-language theorem statement into a LEAN 4 definition. We wrote detailed few-shot prompts that walk the model through one process: summarize the statement, list its variables and hypotheses, formalize each piece against the Mathlib library, gather the required definitions into a file header, and compile-check the result, stepping back when compilation fails.

My contributions

I worked on the LEAN 4 and theorem-proving research that grounded the technical approach, and I helped design the tool-augmented AF method. On the web application I contributed to a single-page React client on React Router with a Flask backend, Google sign-in through OAuth, token-based authentication, a streaming chat interface, and a link out to a live LEAN editor for running generated code. The models reached two LangChain tools: Loogle, a search engine over Mathlib, and a live LEAN environment through a JSON-RPC fork of LeanREPL, so they could look up identifiers and confirm that a theorem compiles.

Retrieval augmentation

To raise formalization quality, we built a vector database over Mathlib for retrieval. The pipeline split the Mathlib4 codebase into roughly 125,000 chunks of 1,024 tokens with 128 tokens of overlap, using OpenAI's tiktoken tokenizer, then embedded each chunk with the text-embedding-ada-002 model into a FAISS index. At query time the system embeds the user's input, pulls the five closest Mathlib chunks, and adds them to the prompt, which helps resolve ambiguous terms and shape correct LEAN structure, types, and headers.

Evaluation and results

We tested on ProofNet, a benchmark of college-level mathematics, using two measures: 1) symbolic equivalence (a formal LEAN proof that two formalizations imply each other), and 2) semantic consistency (the cosine similarity between their re-informalized statements). Few-shot examples plus tool access raised compiling propositions from 30.4% to 70.2% on the validation split. Across 186 test problems, Gemini 2.0 Flash led with 30% of theorems compiling and 10% proven fully equivalent, and it outperformed the reasoning model o4-mini, probably because non-reasoning models follow instructions more closely.

LLMsLEAN 4LangChainRAGTool Use
Deep Learning

FSM Anomaly Detection

View on GitHub

A complete pipeline for catching runtime faults in finite state machines. A small dense autoencoder learns only from traces of normal behavior, then flags any state sequence whose reconstruction error passes a learned threshold. Its accuracy is measured directly against a stateless transition-whitelist baseline.

The problem

Finite state machines control sequential logic throughout digital hardware, including traffic controllers, communication protocols, and on-chip security blocks. At runtime the state register can be corrupted by stuck-at faults, single-event upsets, fault-injection attacks, or random perturbation, and offline test methods often miss these failures. The project asks whether an autoencoder trained only on normal traces can catch such faults as they happen. It then measures that detector against a rule that checks each consecutive state pair against the legal transition table.

What I built

I implemented three reference FSMs of increasing complexity: a four-state traffic light, a five-state detector for the bit pattern 1011, and a six-state vending machine. Around them I built a synthetic data-generation pipeline, three fault injectors (stuck-at, transition, and perturbation), the autoencoder with its training and threshold selection, and a full evaluation harness. The pipeline also produces the figures and tables for a paper: state diagrams, reconstruction-error histograms, receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curves, and the results and sensitivity tables.

Method

Each state is one-hot encoded to avoid implying a false ordering, then sliced into sliding windows of length N. A symmetric dense autoencoder (128, 64, 16 bottleneck, 64, 128) trains with binary cross-entropy, the correct loss for one-hot inputs, using Adam over 50 epochs, with one model per FSM. The detection threshold sits at the 99th percentile of the validation reconstruction error, which targets a false-positive rate near 1% suited to a hardware monitor. The whitelist baseline flags any window that contains an illegal consecutive state pair.

Results

The autoencoder reaches perfect detection on the periodic traffic light. On the vending machine it closes the recall gap for stuck-at faults, catching 94.1% of them against the whitelist's 74.4%, because a frozen legal state still forms a legal pair that the whitelist accepts. On the bit-pattern detector the whitelist's perfect precision keeps it ahead on F1, yet the autoencoder's area under the curve stays between 0.87 and 0.98, which shows the F1 gap comes mostly from threshold choice. A window-size sweep shows the bit-pattern detector improving steadily as windows grow.

Limitations

Each trained model fits only the FSM whose traces it saw, much like a per-asset anomaly detector in industry; the method transfers, but an individual model does not. Every experiment ran on simulated traces, which lack the electrical noise, jitter, and metastability of real registers. Sliding-window detection also adds latency of up to N timesteps, and the percentile threshold trades false-positive rate against sensitivity.

PyTorchAutoencoderAnomaly DetectionNumPyscikit-learn
